schools.summary: Matthew Henry reads the siege as the fatal fruit of Zedekiah's rebellion, Jerusalem's shield of divine favour withdrawn so that innumerable evils compass her about.
Ellicott fixes the exact date against the parallels in Jeremiah and Ezekiel and explains the 'forts' as a siege-work later kept as fast-days in exile.
Wesley reads 'came' and 'built' as chastisement for rebellion and perjury, and Clarke frames the whole chapter's ruin in advance.
Gill notes the account from here agrees exactly with the fuller record of Jeremiah.
Keil treats the verse as the heading of the whole destruction, and Ellicott unravels Nebuzaradan's name and office and the scribal problem in the date.
Clarke gives the two-day timetable of entry and burning; Wesley reads the month's delay as one more offer of God's patience.
Matthew Henry finds mercy in the interval — a space to repent, refused.
Gill notes only the same discrepancy of the day, referring to the harmonizing note on Jeremiah.

debates.question: How is the 'seventh day' here to be reconciled with the 'tenth day' of Jeremiah?

sides.position: a scribal error, the 'tenth' of Jeremiah being correct
both dates are real — Nebuzaradan entered on the seventh and set the fire on the tenth

schools.summary: Gill names the pillars Jachin and Boaz and relays, with scepticism, a Roman relic-legend about them, noting the parallel with Jeremiah's fuller account.
Ellicott marks where Jeremiah 39 ceases to run parallel and reads 'brass' as copper; Keil explains the colossal furniture was shattered because it could not be moved whole.

outliers.note: Relays, with evident scepticism, Benjamin of Tudela's legend that Jachin and Boaz stood in a Roman church and sweated moisture yearly on the ninth of Ab.

schools.summary: Gill explains the 'second priest' as the sagan or deputy who officiated for the high priest.
Clarke agrees Zephaniah was this sagan, a kind of deputy high priest.
Ellicott adds the genealogy of Seraiah (ancestor of Ezra) and the rank of the door-keepers, all the chief temple officials taken together.
Matthew Henry sees a double justice — the king's revenge singling out leaders, and God's justice the ringleaders in idolatry.
Keil traces Gedaliah's lineage and his trust with the Chaldeans; Ellicott vindicates his honour as a disciple of Jeremiah, no traitor.
Clarke calls his office an overseer's, not a king's; Wesley names him a righteous man and friend of Jeremiah.
Matthew Henry sets him as a good governor with the prophet for a privy-council, who might have blessed the remnant more than many kings.
Gill notes the scattered captains submitted to him for a time.
Gill fixes the time — the seventh month of the year, some fifty-two days after the city's fall.
Keil recounts the treacherous murder at a hospitable meal, incited by Baalis and foretold to a disbelieving Gedaliah; Ellicott finds the motive in Ishmael's royal claim.
Clarke enlarges the widening slaughter and Ishmael's flight; Wesley traces the crime to envy of a 'mean' man's advancement.
Keil narrates the sequel — Ishmael's rout by Johanan and the flight to Egypt against Jeremiah's word; Ellicott notes the prophet was taken down with them.
Gill lists the king's daughters and the prophets Jeremiah and Baruch among the fugitives.
Wesley concludes they mingled with the Egyptians and were heard of no more as Israelites.
Matthew Henry presses the folly — they forsook their own good land and so made a full end of themselves, the last threat of Deuteronomy fulfilled.

themes: a full end by their own folly

schools.summary: Gill reckons Jehoiachin now fifty-five years old.
Keil explains the 'lifting of the head' as release and honour, an act of grace at the new king's accession; Ellicott supplies the chronicle of Evil-merodach's brief reign.
Clarke sets the release at Nebuchadnezzar's death, the captive treated as a king's friend; Wesley resolves the discrepancy of the dates.
Keil reckons the siege at eighteen months and Ellicott at one year, five months, and twenty-seven days, both noting the brief lifting of the siege when Hophra of Egypt approached.
Clarke tells how Nebuchadnezzar routed the Egyptians, returned, and pressed the siege until famine and a breach opened the city.
Keil traces the famine to Zedekiah's refusal of Jeremiah's counsel and restores the dropped month from Jeremiah; Ellicott sets the horrors among the laments of Lamentations and Ezekiel.
Wesley marks that it was the common people who first starved, driven even to eat their own children while the king's heart stayed hardened.

debates.question: Does 'the people of the land' mean the civil population or the militia?

sides.position: the civil population of the city, swollen by refugees from the country

schools.summary: Ellicott reconstructs the breach on the north side and the escape through the gate by the king's garden, and Keil carries the flight to its discovery and the capture in the steppes of Jericho, both citing Ezekiel's foresight.
Keil and Ellicott explain the trial at Riblah, whither Nebuchadnezzar had withdrawn, and gloss 'gave judgment' as a solemn arraignment of Zedekiah as a rebel and oath-breaker.
Wesley notes why Nebuchadnezzar stayed at Riblah and that his officers examined the king's cause and passed sentence.
Keil calls the sentence the just reward of Zedekiah's perjury and hardening, and Ellicott adds that blinding was a Babylonian punishment that made good Ezekiel's riddle.
Wesley draws out the cruelty of slaying the children as the king's last sight, a sign that the kingdom was at an end.
Keil and Ellicott read 'every great house' as a limiting phrase, the common houses spared for the poor left behind.

Matthew Henry turns the burning temple into a lesson that God regards little the outward pomp of worship when its life and power are gone, and that a shrine trusted in his place becomes a refuge of lies.
Wesley strikes the same note and dismisses the legend of the hidden ark.

outliers.note: Dismisses as a groundless tale the apocryphal story that Jeremiah rescued and hid the ark on Mount Nebo, since the prophet was then a close prisoner.

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott note the whole army pulling down the walls round about and settle a small textual point about the preposition.
Clarke gathers the whole plunder into this moment and dates Judah's removal centuries from David's reign.
Matthew Henry finds the moral that sin unwalls a people and takes away their defence, the walls unrepaired until Nehemiah.
Keil and Ellicott note the poorest left as vinedressers and plowmen, with a rare Hebrew word for the husbandmen.
Wesley marks the reversal — the rich imprisoned abroad, the poor left at peace at home — as providence humbling the proud.
Matthew Henry observes that poverty is sometimes a protection, for those with nothing have nothing to lose.
Keil and Ellicott catalogue the ministering vessels of the brazen altar, noting Jeremiah's fuller list.
Matthew Henry reads their removal as the end of the ministration itself — those who slighted God's worship and preferred many altars shall now have none.

Keil and Ellicott identify the condemned officers — the garrison commander, the royal counsellors, the muster-scribe, and the sixty men of the citadel — Ellicott taking 'the city' as the city of David.
Clarke faces the five-versus-seven discrepancy and offers his reconciliations.

debates.question: Were there five men of the king's presence, or seven?

sides.position: five originally, Jeremiah's seven reckoning in the war-officer and the scribe (or a textual change)
the numeral 'five' was partly obliterated in the manuscript behind Jeremiah's 'seven'

schools.summary: Keil notes the captives led north to Riblah in the land of Hamath, the same station where Zedekiah had been tried.
Matthew Henry feels the cruelty of a reprieve that was none — men spared and marched far, only to be slain.
Keil lists the captains who gathered to Gedaliah, and Ellicott fills in their histories — marking Ishmael's royal blood — and explains why Mizpah, an ancient sanctuary, was chosen.
Clarke notes only the uncertainty of Mizpah's location.
Keil and Ellicott read the throne 'above the kings' as a place of higher honour, not a loftier seat, among the dethroned monarchs kept at Babylon's court, comparing Cyrus and Croesus.

outliers.note: Illustrates the honour shown Jehoiachin with the Black Prince's chivalrous treatment of his captive, John of France.

themes: the throne set above other kings

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott read the changed garments and the daily place at the royal table as the tokens of a permanent reversal of shame, echoing Joseph raised from prison and Mephibosheth at David's table.
Ellicott takes the continual allowance as a faint gleam of light foreshadowing the pity later shown the captive people; Keil notes the historian follows Jehoiachin to the very end of his life.
Clarke follows Jehoiachin's regal style to his probable death with Evil-merodach; Wesley draws the comfort that none should say they will never see good again.

themes: one steady light at the close

schools.summary: Clarke sweeps the sequel into one grim line and shows how two seemingly contrary prophecies — that Zedekiah should see the king of Babylon yet not see Babylon — were reconciled at once.
Ellicott maps the widening Arabah near Jericho where the king was run to ground, and Keil marks the pathos of an army melting away so that he stood alone.
Clarke explains the king's severity against the counsellors of revolt; Wesley reckons Judah's tenure of the land ended after some eight and a half centuries.
Ellicott takes 'so Judah was carried away' as the epitaph rounding off the whole account, echoing the verdict once passed on the northern kingdom.
Matthew Henry reads the slaughter and deportation as the fulfilment of the covenant threats of Deuteronomy and Amos.
Keil explains the captains' fear and Gedaliah's reassuring oath; Ellicott sharpens the wording and its promise of immunity for the past.
Clarke marks the solemnity of the pledge; Wesley grounds it in the double word of the king of Babylon and of God through Jeremiah, then laments how soon it was dashed to pieces.
Keil distinguishes the three groups deported and weighs (then rejects) the 'remnant of the artisans' reading; Ellicott glosses the deserters and the 'shouting throng.'
Wesley numbers the exiles at eight hundred and thirty-two, the settled townsfolk, with the country people beside them.
Matthew Henry notes most had already perished or scattered, so that only this small remnant was left to be led away.
Ellicott takes the clause as a sweeping seizure of all gold and silver, much of it hidden by the priests at the first plundering; Keil notes the account is abridged from Jeremiah's fuller list.
Matthew Henry observes that what gold and silver remained was now swept away, leaving the house utterly bare.
Ellicott reads 'without weight' as a natural hyperbole like the Assyrian boasts of plunder; Keil lists the colossal objects Solomon had made, broken up for carriage.
Matthew Henry seizes on the immeasurable brass — the silver and gold gone before, but the bronze hauled away by the ton.

themes: the mass of a departed glory

schools.summary: Ellicott corrects the capital's height from three cubits to five and glosses the lattice-work and pomegranates; Keil gathers the same measurements into his record of the destroyed temple bronze, both treating the figures as a reverent memorial.
